name: Green Threads / Fibers

x.coordination: Lightweight threads scheduled in user space (or by the runtime) and multiplexed onto a small pool of OS threads (M:N), cooperatively or via runtime preemption.
hazards: Blocking a carrier OS thread can stall many fibers (if not yield-aware)
Debugging / stack traces across the scheduler

def: Runtime- or library-managed threads far cheaper than OS threads, multiplexed many-to-few onto kernel threads to enable huge numbers of concurrent tasks.
problem: OS threads are too heavy (memory + scheduling) to spawn by the hundreds of thousands for high-concurrency I/O workloads.
mechanism: The runtime maintains lightweight stacks/continuations and schedules them onto a small set of carrier OS threads. Blocking operations are intercepted to yield rather than block the carrier, so one core can drive many fibers (e.g. Java virtual threads, Go goroutines).
benefits: Cheap to create by the millions
High I/O concurrency without async colouring
limits: CPU-bound work still needs real parallelism
Native/blocking calls can pin a carrier
failures: A non-yielding blocking call stalling the scheduler
Pinning carriers via synchronized / FFI, throttling throughput
whenNot: Pure CPU-bound parallelism (use OS threads / SIMD)
myths: "Green threads give more CPU parallelism" — they multiply concurrency, not cores
